coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive fluorescent nitrobenzene gas sensing by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedded in ZIF-8 nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Balakrishnan, Thivyah
Ang, Wei Lun
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a straightforward and highly sensitive luminescence gas sensor designed for the detection of nitrobenzene in gaseous state. The sensor utilizes an innovative composite composed of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (nGQDs) as luminescent materials, successfully encapsulated within the stable porous carrier zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The resulting nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite showed highly sensitive luminescence sensing towards nitrobenzene gas, with a wide linear detection range from 8 to 320 ppm, and the detection limits of sensor as low as 0.44 ppm. Moreover, the nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor demonstrated enhanced ppm-level sensitivity and long-term stability for nitrobenzene detection at room temperature, while maintaining high stability under varying humidity levels and exposure to high temperatures. This enhancement is attributed to the increased contact area provided by ZIF-8 matrix, facilitating adsorption of nitrobenzene molecules on nGQDs. The nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibited good selectivity towards nitrobenzene gas (57 %) compared to other nitro volatile organic compound (VOC) vapors (nitromethane and nitropropane), and almost no significant responses were observed for the other tested VOC vapors (methanol, acetone, formaldehyde, benzene). This can be attributed to the strong interaction between the electron-deficient nitrobenzene and the nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. Overall, the proposed gas sensor in this study showed high potential for a simple, efficient, and reliable detection of nitrobenzene gas in environmental monitoring.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive fluorescent nitrobenzene gas sensing by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedded in ZIF-8 nanocomposite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a straightforward and highly sensitive luminescence gas sensor designed for the detection of nitrobenzene in gaseous state. The sensor utilizes an innovative composite composed of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (nGQDs) as luminescent materials, successfully encapsulated within the stable porous carrier zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The resulting nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite showed highly sensitive luminescence sensing towards nitrobenzene gas, with a wide linear detection range from 8 to 320 ppm, and the detection limits of sensor as low as 0.44 ppm. Moreover, the nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor demonstrated enhanced ppm-level sensitivity and long-term stability for nitrobenzene detection at room temperature, while maintaining high stability under varying humidity levels and exposure to high temperatures. This enhancement is attributed to the increased contact area provided by ZIF-8 matrix, facilitating adsorption of nitrobenzene molecules on nGQDs. The nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibited good selectivity towards nitrobenzene gas (57 %) compared to other nitro volatile organic compound (VOC) vapors (nitromethane and nitropropane), and almost no significant responses were observed for the other tested VOC vapors (methanol, acetone, formaldehyde, benzene). This can be attributed to the strong interaction between the electron-deficient nitrobenzene and the nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. Overall, the proposed gas sensor in this study showed high potential for a simple, efficient, and reliable detection of nitrobenzene gas in environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrialization and active growth in the economy has led to serious environmental issues, especially air pollution. One of the major contributors to air pollution is emission of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) vapors from chemical, petrochemical, and allied industries [1–5]. Nitrobenzene is a potent contaminant extensively utilized as a crucial organic intermediate in the manufacturing of aniline, pharmaceuticals, lubricating oils, dyes, synthetic rubber, and [6,7]. Due to the high volatility of nitrobenzene, they readily volatilize into the atmosphere at room temperature, giving rise to the formation of toxic VOC, contributing to air pollution. Up to date, the conventional methods used for VOCs vapor detection are gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, ion mobility spectrometry, colorimetric detector and so on [8–11]. While these methods offer high sensitivity and accurate detection, many of them require expensive instruments and specialized expertise, making the process time-consuming. The electrochemical detection method, despite its high sensitivity, faces challenges with weak selectivity, particularly in electrochemical sensors using metal oxides that require elevated operating temperatures. However, there is a strong urge to come up with a simple, reliable, and effective sensor for detection of nitrobenzene vapor at room temperature with the use of advanced technologies of electronics and optics.

GQDs exhibit outstanding properties such as impressive optical clarity, extensive surface area, distinctive photoluminescence, compatibility with biological systems, low toxicity, strong chemical stability, and the ability to tailor their energy band gap according to specific needs [12–14]. Additionally, due to their extremely small particle size, GQDs provide numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, vacancies, and defects, making them a favored option for applications in gas sensing. However, GQDs are susceptible to light and temperature, and they demonstrate weak chemical stability in real environmental conditions [15]. Consequently, the rapid deterioration of the photoluminescence efficiency and electrical properties of GQDs poses limitations on their long-term performance and reliability. Addressing this stability challenge is imperative to fully unleash the potential of GQDs and realizing their practical applications. The controlled embedding of GQDs within porous structures such as MOFs, can prevent their aggregation, ensuring long-term stability [16–18]. It is essential to investigate methods for integrating GQDs into MOF matrices that are both designable and tunable, imparting multifunctional properties to GQDs. However, in the development of an optimal GQDs@MOF nanosensor for diverse sensing applications, it is critical to construct the GQDs@MOF nanocomposite in a way that preserves the original physical–chemical characteristics and configurations of the GQDs and MOFs.

Moreover, scientists have actively investigated methods to adjust the chemical characteristics of GQDs by incorporating elements and altering the functional groups along their edges and surfaces. This involves the modification of groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amino groups, with the goal of improving their capabilities in gas sensing [13,19]. Zhang et al. studied the NO2 gas sensing properties of nitrogen doped-GQDs@ZnO nanocomposite, synthesized through hydrothermal methods [20]. The nanocomposite demonstrated a notable 11.6-fold improvement in sensitivity at 5 ppm NO2, reaching a detection limit of 0.1 ppm, while also effectively lowering the operational temperature from 160 °C to 100 °C. The higher sensitivity to NO2 is attributed to the doping of electronegative nitrogen (N) atoms, where the electron-attracting NO2 molecules preferentially bind to the N atoms due to the basicity of N-containing groups. In addition to incorporating functionalized GQDs, researchers have integrated them with specific materials like metal oxides [21,22] and polymers [23] to fulfill the specific requirements of gas-sensing applications. For instance, Lee et al. successfully decorated SnO2 with GQDs (GQD@SnO2) to enhance the detection of NO2 across a broad temperature range, from room temperature to 150 °C [24]. The incorporation of GQDs not only led to a 30-fold increase in the response to NO2 at 150 °C compared to pristine SnO2 nanodomes but also notably improved the recovery time, reducing it from 1247 s for the bare SnO2 nanodomes. Indium et al. developed a novel ternary nanocomposite utilizing the conducting polymer PANI, hollow In2O3 nanofiber, and N-GQD for NH3 gas sensing through an in situ chemical oxidative polymerization [25]. The PANI/N-GQD/ hollow In2O3 nanofiber sensor demonstrated a response of 15.2 when exposed to 1 ppm NH3, indicating an enhancement of more than 4.4 times compared to the PANI sensor.

In this study, we present nitrogen doped GQDs (nGQDs) embedded within zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8), known as nGQD@ZIF-8 for a highly efficient nitrobenzene gas sensor at room temperature. ZIF-8, a type of MOF, has garnered significant attention due to its microporosity, a large surface area, and suitable pore cavities accommodating luminescence units, along with its broad structural diversity, make it favorable for nanocomposite formations and applications in chemical sensing [26–28]. The incorporation of ZIF-8 can prevent the agglomeration of nGQDs, and the composite material offers abundant adsorption sites for nitrobenzene gas, thereby facilitating electron transmission and further enhancing the gas sensing properties of the nGQD@ZIF-8 composite. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the employment of nGQD@ZIF-8 composite as luminescence gas sensor material. The nGQDs, ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 were characterized using different techniques including TEM, SEM, PL spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, cyclic voltammetry characterization and thermogravimetric analysis. Furthermore, the key gas sensing parameters including sensitivity, selectivity, humidity, exposure temperature and sensor stability were investigated for luminescence sensing of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 towards nitrobenzene gas.

Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %) and triethylamine (TEA, 99 %) were supplied from Fisher Chemical in the UK and Friendemann Schmidt in Malaysia, respectively. Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), 2-Methylimidazole (2-MeIM, 99 %) and Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O, 99.5 %) were provided by Sigma Aldrich, sourced from France, India, and Germany, respectively. Potassium chloride (KCl, 99 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 99.8 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 99.8 %), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99 %),) were purchased from E. Merck (Germany). The VOC solvents, such as methanol (Merck, 99.9 %), acetone (Merck, 99.8 %), formaldehyde (Merck, 99.8 %), benzene (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.99 %), nitromethane, nitropropane and nitrobenzene (Sigma Aldrich, > 99.7 %) were used for test gas generation. All chemicals were used without further purification. In addition, ultrahigh-purity nitrogen (Air Gas, purity of 99.999 %) was used as a diluent and carrier gas. Whatman Grade 41 filter paper (Whatman, Kent, England) was used as solid support for fabrication of PL sensor.

The nGQDs were synthesized using a direct pyrolysis method with slight modification [29]. Initially, 2 g of citric acid (CA) and 636 µL of ethylenediamine (EDA), maintaining a molar ratio of CA to EDA at 1.0:1.0, were mixed in a round-bottom flask. The mixture was then heated to 200 °C for 7 min. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solid product was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized (DI) water, and the obtained nGQDs solution was filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 µm) to eliminate any large fragments. Finally, the brown solution containing nGQDs was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The nGQD@ZIF-8 composites were synthesized using a bottle-around-ship strategy. The synthesis procedure is outlined as follows. Firstly, 2-MeIM (3.31 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of DI water and subsequently, 3 mL of TEA was added while stirring. Concurrently, 2 g of Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O was dissolved in 12 mL of DI water under constant stirring (200 rpm). The solutions, along with 25 mL of nGQDs solution, were combined and vigorously stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation (7000 rpm for 20 min) and rinsed with DI water three times to eliminate excess nGQDs before being dried overnight at 65 °C. The ZIF-8 was synthesized using the same procedure, excluding the addition of nGQDs, as per a previously reported literature [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nGQDs, ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature using a single-beam spectrofluorophotometer (Edinburg Instrument, UK). Sample morphologies were examined through transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Fisher-Talos 120C, USA) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM with EDX, Zeiss Merlin, Germany). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer, with a 2θ angle scan range from 0° to 50°. Raman spectra were acquired through Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, Model: DXR2xi) at 532 nm. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas and porosities were analyzed using a nitrogen adsorption instrument (Micromeritics Tristar II Plus, USA) after overnight degassing at 180 °C. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments were conducted on a Shimadzu TGA 50 under a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min, covering the temperature range from 25 to 800 °C. UV viewing cabinet (Vilber Laurmat, France) served as the UV light source in this study. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) characterization was caried out using the Metrohm AUTOLAB Potentiostat/Galvanostat with NOVA 2.1.4 software (METROHM, Switzerland). The electrolytic medium for the electrochemical cell, a 0.1 M KCl supporting electrolyte solution, was prepared using analytical grade reagents without further purification. All electrochemical measurements were conducted using a 3-electrode system, comprising bare glass carbon electrodes (GCEs) and modified GCEs (ZIF-8/GCE, nGQDs/GCE, nGQD@ZIF-8/GCE) with a diameter of 4 mm as the working electrode (WS), a platinum wire (1 mm in diameter) as the auxiliary electrode (AU), and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE) in 3 M KCl.

The prepared nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 samples were deposited onto filter paper using the drop casting method, as shown in Fig. 1 . Each of the samples was dispersed into DI water and sonicated to obtain a homogenous solution with a concentration of 1 mg mL−1. Filter papers of 5 mm diameter were prepared. Then, 10 µL of each sample was drop casted onto the paper strips and dried at 60 °C for 30 min. The visual images of the paper strips were taken under 365 nm UV radiation using an iPhone 13 equipped with a 12-mega- pixel rear camera. The prepared paper strips kept at room temperature for the subsequent gas sensing test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of homemade gas sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
The gas sensing properties of the sensors were evaluated by a homemade test system at room temperature in a dynamic manner (continuous gas flow), as illustrated in Fig. 2 and the actual setup is shown in Fig. A1. The test VOC solvents included methanol, acetone, formaldehyde, benzene, nitromethane, nitropropane, and nitrobenzene. Each targeted VOC liquid was contained within a bubbler system, and N2 gas served as the carrier to flow the VOC vapor to the test chamber. Different concentrations of the tested VOCs were achieved by diluting them with N2 gas, controlling the gas flow rates with two gas flowmeters. These flow rates were adjusted based on the vapor pressure of the VOC, while ensuring that the total flow through the chamber remained constant at 200 cm3 min−1. The concentration (C) of the generated gas from liquid VOC calculated using the following equation [31]:

Where PV is the vapor pressure of the VOC (mm Hg), PT is the total pressure (760 mm Hg), F1 and F2 are the flowrates (cm3 min−1) of the N2 as carrier gas and the bubbling saturated VOC vapor, respectively. For example: 150 cm3 min−1 of N2 gas and 50 cm3 min−1 of nitrobenzene vapor (kept at room temperature) with a saturated vapor pressure of 0.245 mm Hg result in an 80 ppm concentration. The vapor pressure of each test gas is presented in Table A1.

The initial PL emission intensities of the nGQDs-, ZIF-8- and nGQD@ZIF-8 embedded paper strips were measured before exposed to the desired VOC vapors. Then, the prepared paper strip was placed into the test chamber and kept in contact with different test VOC vapors (methanol, acetone, formaldehyde, benzene, nitromethane, nitropropane, and nitrobenzene). For the gas sensing measurement, the target VOC gas with constant concentration of 300 ppm was continuously introduced into test chamber (Valves 1, 2, 4: open and Valve 3: closed). After 5 min, the gas flow in the test chamber was stopped (Valve 4: closed) and the paper strip then taken out from test chamber. The PL emission spectrum of the paper strip after VOC vapor exposure was recorded immediately. The sensor material's response to a specific analyte was evaluated by observing changes in the PL spectra of the sample after exposure to the VOC vapor. The sensor response was calculated as below:
(2)

Where I0 is the initial maximum peak PL intensity of the sensor, and I is the maximum peak PL intensity after exposure to test VOC vapor.
a.
Interference study

To assess the selectivity of the sensing materials, the response of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 paper strips towards various VOC gases such as methanol, acetone, formaldehyde, benzene, nitromethane, and nitropropane in the presence of nitrobenzene gas at 320 ppm was monitored. The gas mixture was continuously introduced into the test chamber (refer Fig. 2; Valves 1, 2, 3, and 4: open). After 5 min, the gas flow in the test chamber was stopped, and the paper strip was then removed. The PL emission spectrum of the paper strip after exposure to VOC vapors was recorded immediately.
b.
Humidity

To investigate the impact of humidity on sensor performance, six relative humidity (RH) levels were examined: 0 %, 15 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 90 %, maintaining a total flow rate of 200 cm3 min−1. Nitrobenzene at a concentration of 80 ppm was introduced to the sensor strip under varying RH conditions for 4 min. The corresponding PL spectra were recorded for each RH level, and the sensor responses were calculated using Equation (2).
c.
Temperature

The influence of temperature on gas sensing performance of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 paper strip was evaluated by measuring the change in PL intensity under various temperature conditions. The test chamber was immersed in a hot oil bath maintained at temperature ranging from 20 to 90 °C for 15 min. Subsequently, 80 ppm nitrobenzene was introduced to the sensor strip under varying temperature conditions for 4 min. The corresponding PL spectra were recorded for each temperature, and the sensor responses were calculated using Equation (2).
d.
Sensor stability

The influence of storage duration on the gas sensing performance of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 paper strips was investigated by measuring the change in PL intensity over a 3-month period. The PL intensity of the samples was measured twice a month after exposure to 80 ppm nitrobenzene vapor for 4 min, allowing for the monitoring of any changes in the sensor strips' performance. PL spectra were recorded for each storage duration, and sensor responses were calculated using Equation (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nGQDs, ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of nGQDs, ZIF-8, and nGQD@ZIF-8 was studied using TEM and SEM imaging techniques. As can be seen in Fig. 3 a, the nGQDs demonstrated well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 4.85 nm. ZIF-8 nanoparticles displayed a rhombic dodecahedron morphology with an average diameter of 165 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b, consistent with existing literature [32,33]. The TEM image of the resulting nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite revealed a rhombic dodecahedron structure similar to ZIF-8 (Fig. 3c), with an average size of approximately 180 nm. The HRTEM image of nGQD@ZIF-8 (Fig. 3d) displays that the highly dispersed nGQDs are wrapped at the edges of the ZIF-8 nanoparticle, indicating successful encapsulation within the ZIF-8 matrix. Additionally, the HRTEM reveals a fringe spacing value of 0.310 nm for nGQDs (Fig. A2), corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphitic carbon within nGQDs [34]. The SEM images distinctly illustrate the materials, indicating a nearly identical hexagonal shape in both ZIF-8 (Fig. 3e) and nGQD@ZIF-8 (Fig. 3f). This similarity in size and shape suggests that the incorporation of nGQDs does not alter the overall morphology of bare ZIF-8.

The elemental composition of nGQDs, ZIF-8, and nGQD@ZIF-8 was studied using EDX analysis. The EDX spectra shows the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and N in nGQDs (Fig. 4 a) and Zinc (Zn), C, O, and N in ZIF-8 (Fig. 4b). The observed spectrum of nGQD@ZIF-8 (Fig. 4c) showed strong peaks for C, O, N and Zn, demonstrating the successful formation of the nanocomposite. The data about the weight percentages obtained for C, N, O, and Zn is given in the inset of the spectrums. Moreover, the distribution of various elements was relatively uniform in SEM mapping of nGQD@ZIF-8 (Fig. A3).

The Raman spectrum of the nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite displays characteristic peaks corresponding to both nGQDs and ZIF-8, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The spectrum of nGQDs presents typical carbon characteristic peaks centered at 1339 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands respectively. The D band arises from local defects and disorders, particularly those located at the edges of graphene and graphite platelets, and the G band originates from sp2 bonds of carbon atoms [35]. However, after encapsulation within ZIF-8, the peak centers of nGQDs shift to 1351 and 1592 cm−1, indicating a potential strong interaction between nGQDs and ZIF-8 [36]. Concurrently, the Raman peaks of ZIF-8 align with previous reports, featuring strong peaks at 691 cm−1, 1145 cm−1, and 1454 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of the imidazole ring, C–N stretching vibration, and methyl bending vibration, respectively. The stretching vibration of the C–H in the methyl group occurs at 2923 cm−1, and the C–H stretching vibration on the imidazole ring emerges at 3124 cm−1. Furthermore, the peaks of nGQD@ZIF-8 closely match those of pristine ZIF-8 with no observable peak shifts, indicating the absence of significant structural defects in ZIF-8 after nGQDs encapsulation.

The XRD pattern of nGQDs exhibits a wide peak with a center around ∼ 23°, as shown in Fig. 5b. This particular peak is attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the graphitic characteristics of nGQDs with extensively disordered carbon atoms [37]. From Fig. 5b, it can be observed that both ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 display a reflection at 2θ = 7.5°, corresponding to a 110 peak. This indicates that both nanocrystals possess a rhombic dodecahedron shape with 12 facets [38], aligning well with the TEM images. In comparison to the simulated ZIF-8 pattern, distinct diffraction peaks assigned to pristine ZIF-8 are evident in both the synthesized ZIF-8 matrix and nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. This observation strongly supports that the incorporation of nGQDs does not affect the crystalline integrity of ZIF-8. The maintenance of the structural integrity of ZIF-8 is probably attributed to the low concentrations and small sizes of the encapsulated nGQDs within the ZIF-8 crystals [39,40]. The XRD patterns of nGQD@ZIF-8 display a broad peak within the 18°-28° (2θ) range, corresponding to the diffraction peak observed in the case of nGQDs.

Fig. 6 a and 6b illustrate the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the nGQD@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8, respectively. Both materials exhibit type IV isotherms, indicating that they possess multi-stage pore structures with a combination of micropores and mesopores, as per the IUPAC classification [41]. The fact that ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 share the same type of isotherm suggests that the structure of ZIF-8 remains unchanged following the introduction of nGQDs, aligning with the findings from the XRD data. The BET surface area of nGQD@ZIF-8 (532.54 m2 g−1) is lower than that of pure ZIF-8 (582.67 m2 g−1), indicating a decrease in surface area due to the incorporation of nonporous nGQDs with loading amount of 9.42 wt% into ZIF-8.

The thermal stability of ZIF-8, nGQDs, and nGQD@ZIF-8 were further investigated using TGA, as depicted in Fig. 6c. The TGA profile of nGQDs reveals three decomposition steps. The initial weight loss of nGQDs at around 140 °C is attributed to the removal of moisture content. At 215 °C, the subsequent weight reduction observed, which corresponds to the elimination of oxygen-containing surface functional groups [42]. The final decomposition of nGQDs occurs at 315 °C due to the breakdown of the carbons into CO2 gas [37]. The thermogram of ZIF-8 exhibits clear degradation temperatures at 265 °C and 390 °C, indicating a higher level of thermal stability compared to nGQDs. This difference in stability can be linked to nGQDs containing relatively less stable oxygen groups, such as hydroxyl groups. For the nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite, stability is maintained up to 245 °C, evident from the lower percentage of weight loss for nGQD@ZIF-8 (27 %) compared to nGQDs (18 %) and similar to ZIF-8 when subjected to a temperature of 600 °C. The enhanced thermal stability of nGQD@ZIF-8 is a result of the interactions between the ZIF-8 and nGQDs.

Furthermore, the dispersion of nGQD@ZIF-8, when viewed under natural light, exhibits a milky-white appearance and shows intense blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 7 a). As shown in Fig. 7b, the PL emission occurs at 465 nm upon excitation at 365 nm, similar to the emission profile of nGQDs. Notably, pure ZIF-8 does not display a distinct peak at 465 nm, confirming that the blue fluorescence observed in nGQD@ZIF-8 originates from the nGQDs rather than the ZIF-8 crystals.

The gas sensing response measurements were conducted at room temperature (25 °C). Initially, the optimal exposure duration for luminescence sensing of nitrobenzene vapor was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibited a shorter response time (3 mins) and the response maintained stable at prolonged duration (>3 min) when exposed to 80 ppm nitrobenzene vapor in contrast to nGQDs (5 mins). Consequently, exposure durations of 3 mins and 5 mins were selected as the best durations for subsequent PL gas sensing experiments with nGQD@ZIF-8 and nGQDs, respectively. The incorporation of nGQDs within ZIF-8 provides higher surface area, offering more active sites for gas adsorption and contributing to a quicker response [43].

The response of both nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 to different concentrations of nitrobenzene was also investigated. Changes in PL intensity and PL gas response for nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8, exposed to nitrobenzene concentrations ranging from 8 to 320 ppm, are depicted in Fig. A4a-b and Fig. 8b, respectively. The visual photos of nGQD@ZIF8 paper strips before and after exposure to nitrobenzene vapor can be referred to in Fig. A4c. It is evident from Fig. 8b that at low nitrobenzene concentrations (8 ppm), nGQDs show no response. In contrast, the gas response of nGQD@ZIF-8 increases with the concentration of nitrobenzene. Notably, the nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor exhibited a higher response of 64 % compared to bare nGQDs (43 %) when exposed to 320 ppm nitrobenzene vapor. This observation can be attributed to the presence of ZIF-8, which provides higher electron transfer pathways and contributes to increased surface area and adsorption sites [44], as illustrated in Fig. 9 .

The linear fitted plots of gas response for nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors versus concentration of nitrobenzene vapor were used to study the relationship between gas response and gas concentration, as illustrated in Fig. 10 a. The sensitivity of the sensors was estimated by measuring the slopes of the fitted plots, which is defined as ‘the change of a measured signal per analyte concentration unit’. Therefore, it can be observed that the sensitivities of nGQD@ZIF-8 as a luminescence gas sensor (0.473) was higher than those of bare nGQDs (0.318). The theoretical limit of detection (LOD) is presented according to the following equation [45]: LOD (ppm) = 3σ/S, where σ and S are the standard deviation and the sensitivity of the sensor, respectively. The LOD of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 were as low as 10.08 ppm and 0.44 ppm, respectively. The smaller value of LOD for nGQD@ZIF-8 reveals its better potential in more accurate detection of nitrobenzene. The ZIF-8 serves as nitrobenzene vapor pre-concentrator [46], reducing the LOD of nGQD@ZIF-8, which is below the threshold exposure limit of 1 ppm averaged over an 8-hour period proposed by Occupational Safety and Health Administration, Malaysia.

In real-world scenarios, nitrobenzene gas is frequently present together with various other hazardous gases. Thus, selectivity serves as a crucial factor that is commonly employed to evaluate the efficacy of gas sensors under practical conditions. To verify the selectivity of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8, the sensor responses to several VOC vapors, including methanol (M), acetone (AC), formaldehyde (FA), benzene (B), nitromethane (NM), and nitropropane (NP), were measured, as exhibited in Fig. 10b. Both nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors showed the highest response to 80 ppm nitrobenzene (NB) compared to other nitro VOC vapors and almost no significant responses were observed for the remaining VOC vapors (80 ppm). The highly electronegative property of nitrobenzene facilitates the efficient capture of electrons from the p-type sensors (nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8), resulting in a higher sensor response compared to other gases that exhibit lower electronegativity [47].

In addition, the response of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors towards binary gas mixtures: methanol, acetone, formaldehyde, benzene, nitromethane, and nitropropane in the presence of nitrobenzene gas at 80 ppm was monitored, separately. Fig. 10c demonstrate that both sensors exhibited no significant difference in gas responses even in the presence of interfering VOC vapors. These results strongly suggest that nGQD@ZIF-8 is highly selective to nitrobenzene vapor and has the potential for practical applications compared to bare nGQDs due to its higher gas responses in both sensing modes. It can be concluded that nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibits a high affinity for nitrobenzene vapor, while showing negligible reactivity to other vapors. The detailed gas sensing mechanism towards nitrobenzene vapor is explained in section 3.2.3.

To further explore the feasibility of the sensors in practical application, the effects of relative humidity (RH) and exposure temperature on the gas responses were investigated. The impact of RH on the gas sensor needs to be considered, as the presence of water vapor in the air frequently interferes with sensor sensitivity, posing a significant challenge for practical applications. Fig. 11 a shows the influence of RH on 80 ppm nitrobenzene vapor sensing performance of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors. The response values of nGQDs exhibit a 50 % change as RH increases up to 90 %, attributed to its high affinity for moisture, which interrupts the adsorption of nitrobenzene molecules [48]. In contrast, the results indicate that RH in the range of 15–50 RH% has a negligible effect on the gas sensing performance of nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors. At 90 RH%, the response value of nGQD@ZIF-8 showed decrease from 43.56 % to 35.65 %, which was 18.9 % of the original response value. This suggests that the nanocomposite can be applied in real-world environments without concerns about humidity interference. The protective barrier provided by the porous structure and hydrophobic characteristics of ZIF-8 shields nGQDs from moisture exposure, enhancing their moisture stability during gas sensing [49].

Furthermore, the effect of different exposure temperatures ranging from 25 °C to 85 °C on the performance of the gas sensors were exhibited in Fig. 11b. It can be observed that the response values of nGQDs in both modes decreases as the exposure temperature increases from 65 °C to 85 °C. Interestingly, the response values for nGQD@ZIF-8 to 80 ppm nitrobenzene at operating temperatures of 25 °C, 45 °C, 65 °C, and 85 °C are well maintained. This implies that the high thermal stability of ZIF-8 reduces the chances of oxidation or other thermal-induced processes that might degrade the nGQDs [33], preserving their properties and ensuring more reliable gas sensing performance.

To test the stability of the nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors, the gas sensing performance of the sensors was measured every two weeks, as shown in Fig. 11c. The nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors almost maintained the initial response value for 12 weeks, indicating a reliable long-term stability. Furthermore, we characterized nGQD@ZIF-8 using TEM, Raman and XRD after exposure to 80 ppm nitrobenzene at room temperature for 10 min. It was observed that the morphology of the sensor was well maintained (Fig. A5a), the Raman pattern remained identical to that of the fresh nGQD@ZIF-8 (Fig. A5b) and there were no additional impurity peaks in the XRD pattern (Fig. A5c), indicating no obvious crystal structure change occurred. These demonstrated the robust structure and good stability of nGQD@ZIF-8 even after exposure to nitrobenzene vapor. Finally, we studied the reproducibility of nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor since it demonstrated the enhanced gas sensing performance towards nitrobenzene. In Fig. A6, four nGQD@ZIF-8 gas sensors were prepared and tested towards 80 ppm nitrobenzene at room temperature. There is no significant difference between the response values, demonstrating a good reproducibility.

In considering practical applications, it is essential to evaluate the comprehensive performance of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors, taking into account four key indicators. These include the operating temperature coefficient (1/OPT), selectivity factor (SF), sensing kinetics (1/TRes), and sensor response (S). To facilitate the assessment of nitrobenzene sensing performance, a four-axis radar image was presented in Fig. 12 . The extent of coverage in the radar diagram directly correlates with the overall gas-sensitive performance, as demonstrated in previous studies [50]. From Fig. 12, the blue area representing the nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor is significantly larger than that of pure nGQDs (orange area), clearly indicating that the nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor exhibits more balanced and enhanced sensing behaviors compared to the pure nGQDs sensor.

The sensing properties of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 sensors for nitrobenzene detection were compared with the reported data, as shown in Table 1 . Compared to the studies reported by Majeed et al. [45], Cheng et al. [51] and Cui et al. [52], this study presented detailed investigations on crucial parameters for nitrobenzene vapor sensing including sensitivity, selectivity, response time, response stability and so on as discussed in previous sections. Both the nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 luminescence-based gas sensor had the advantages of high sensitivity and short response time at very low concentration (80 ppm) and satisfactory LOD. The enhanced sensing performance of nGQD@ZIF-8 was attributed to large specific surface, abundant mesoporous structure, efficient electron transfer and the synergistic effect of nGQDs and ZIF-8.

The chemical activity of molecules is illustrated by the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), which are intricately linked to the donation and acceptance of electrons within the molecule [22]. The HOMO and LUMO contribute to a crucial parameter known as the energy gap. This energy gap provides insights into the electron transfer, stability, and electrical resistivity exhibited by a structure. The calculated LUMO, HOMO and band gap energies for nGQDs, and nGQD@ZIF-8 from CV data are tabulated in Table 2 . It can be observed that the LUMO, HOMO and band gap energy of nGQD@ZIF-8 are almost similar to that of nGQDs.

To understand the gas sensing mechanism, the molecular orbitals of nGQD@ZIF-8 were compared with the target VOC gases, as shown in Fig. 13 . For luminescence-based sensing mechanism, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process is described. PET process involves electron transfer between LUMO of electron rich excited donor probe and LUMO of the analyte [45,51]. The LUMO energies of methanol (M = 2.10 eV), acetone (AC = 0.18 eV), benzene (B = -0.42 eV), and formaldehyde (FA = 0.34 eV) were significantly higher than that of nGQD@ZIF-8 (nGZ = -3.249 eV). This suggests that the electron transfer from the excited nGQD@ZIF-8 to these test analytes was thermodynamically forbidden, resulting in poor fluorescence responses [52]. In contrast, nitrobenzene exhibited the lowest LUMO energy level (NB = -3.74 eV) among all the analytes tested. In this case, nGQD@ZIF-8 served as an electron donor, while the nitrobenzene molecule acted as an electron acceptor, as illustrated in Fig. 14 . Due to the lower energy level of nitrobenzene's LUMO compared to other analytes, there was a stronger thermodynamic driving force for electron transfer from the nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor to nitrobenzene. This strong tendency for electron transfer resulted in the highest PL quenching observed for nitrobenzene vapor. Consequently, this transfer reduced the number of available electrons for recombination with the holes in the nanocomposite, thereby decreasing the emission of photons and leading to PL quenching when exposed to nitrobenzene vapor. Therefore, nGQD@ZIF-8 demonstrates high selectivity and sensitivity to nitrobenzene compared to the other tested VOCs.

In conclusion, a blue-emissive nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was successfully produced through a straightforward bottle-around-ship method at room temperature. This synthesized nanocomposite not only demonstrates good fluorescence properties attributed mainly to nitrogen doping in GQDs but also preserves the structural integrity of ZIF-8 following encapsulation. The nGQD@ZIF-8 sensor exhibited higher responses of 64 % compared to bare nGQDs (43 %) when exposed to 320 ppm nitrobenzene vapor. The calculated LOD of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 was as low as 10.08 ppm and 0.44 ppm, respectively. Comparative analyses have revealed that the nGQD@ZIF-8 composite sensor outperforms pristine nGQDs in terms of sensitivity, with lower limit of detection. This enhancement is attributed to the increased contact area provided by the ZIF-8 matrix, facilitating the permeability of gas molecules and their effective adsorption on nGQDs. Remarkably, both sensors demonstrated consistent gas responses, showing no significant differences even when exposed to interfering VOC vapors. The incorporation of neural networks and artificial intelligence into nGQD@ZIF-8-based gas sensors are an exciting prospect in the field of gas sensing.

Thivyah Balakrishnan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei Lun Ang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Ebrahim Mahmoudi: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Data curation.
